_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for York County, SC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses.
display_name: York County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: York, SC — 2 S. Congress Street (county seat is the town of York, not Rock Hill)

previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame courthouse. Replaced as county grew.
Brick courthouse. Replaced by larger structure.
Survived the Civil War — Union forces did not systematically damage the courthouse. Served until replaced by the 1914 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: York County's courthouse has survived without catastrophic fire or war damage. Records run continuously from the county's 1785 formation. York is one of the best-documented Upcountry counties. Notably, York County was a focus of the federal Ku Klux Klan trials of 1871–72, and the trial records (held at the National Archives) provide detailed testimony about Reconstruction-era violence that supplements county records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in York County after 1785, the civil records almost certainly survive. The county's proximity to Charlotte means researchers should also check Mecklenburg County, NC, records for cross-border activity.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.repository: York County Register of Deeds; SC Department of Archives and History microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — wills and estate records from near-formation
wills_estates.repository: York County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from formation. Licenses required statewide 1911.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1911 coverage through bonds and church records.
marriages.repository: York County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.repository: SC Department of Archives and History; York County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: SC DHEC; SC Department of Archives and History

substitute_sources.source_type: Springs Industries / Springs Cotton Mills records
substitute_sources.description: Springs Industries (formerly Springs Cotton Mills), headquartered in Fort Mill, was one of the largest privately held textile companies in America. The Springs family maintained extensive business records including payroll, employment, and housing records for the Fort Mill mill complex.
substitute_sources.repository: Springs Close Family Archives (access varies); some records at the Fort Mill History Museum; Louise Pettus Archives at Winthrop University (Rock Hill)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The Springs records are among the most complete textile company archives in the Upcountry. For families who worked at Springs mills in Fort Mill, these records may be more informative than county civil records. Access can be limited due to private family control of some collections.
substitute_sources.source: Hanchett, Thomas W. Sorting Out the New South City. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.

substitute_sources.description: York County was a primary focus of the federal Ku Klux Klan prosecutions of 1871–72. The trial records include detailed testimony from victims, witnesses, and Klan members about Reconstruction-era violence. These records document individuals and events that do not appear in county records.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives and Records Administration, RG 21 (Records of District Courts); published in KKK Testimony Taken by the Joint Select Committee (13 vols., 1872)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for documenting both Black victims and white perpetrators of Reconstruction violence. Testimony names individuals, describes specific events, and provides biographical detail not available from any other source. For Black families in York County during Reconstruction, these trial records may be the most detailed documentation of their lives.
substitute_sources.source: West, Jerry Lee. The Reconstruction Ku Klux Klan in York County, South Carolina. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2002.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. York County is enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. York County's proximity to Charlotte means some family members may appear in Mecklenburg County, NC, census records — check both sides of the state line.

narrative_hooks: York County's courthouse records run unbroken from 1785, but the most revealing documents about {ancestor_name}'s Reconstruction-era experience may be in the federal Ku Klux Klan trial records at the National Archives — testimony that names names and describes violence the county records never recorded.

If {ancestor_name} worked at Springs Cotton Mills in Fort Mill, the company's records — among the most complete in the Upcountry — may document {pronoun_possessive} employment, housing, and daily life in ways the county courthouse never did.

York County sits on the North Carolina border, and {ancestor_name} may appear in records on both sides — check Mecklenburg County, NC, as well, especially after Charlotte's growth began pulling workers across the Catawba River.
South Carolina did not require marriage licenses until 1911 or birth/death registration until 1915. For {ancestor_name} before those dates, church records are the primary substitute.
source_references: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.
West, Jerry Lee. The Reconstruction Ku Klux Klan in York County, South Carolina. Jefferson, NC: McFarland, 2002.
Hanchett, Thomas W. Sorting Out the New South City. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: South Carolina County Records. Columbia, 1937–1942.
